original_abstract:
Single-atom catalysts featuring well-defined metal centers provide a powerful platform for interrogating active site structures and the underlying mechanisms of chemical transformations. However, capturing the structural and electronic dynamics of these sites under real-world reaction conditions remains difficult, largely due to the scarcity of model systems that are both highly accessible and compatible with specialized *operando* probes. 

In this study,เรา employ state-of-the-art *operando* techniques to perform an exhaustive investigation of the dynamic evolution occurring during the electrochemical CO₂ reduction reaction (CO₂RR) on a model catalyst. The system consists of iron centers immobilized as high-spin (HS) Fe(III)N₄ units in their resting state. Through a combination of *operando* ⁵⁷Fe Mössbauer and X-ray absorption spectroscopies, we clearly demonstrate a transition from the initial HS Fe(III)N₄ to a HS Fe(II)N₄ center as the applied potential decreases. Interestingly, the stability of this Fe(II) species and the nature of its adsorbates are heavily influenced by the electrolyte saturation (CO₂ vs. Ar). 

Furthermore, *operando* Raman spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry reveal that the phthalocyanine (Pc) ligand itself participates in the redox process, undergoing a one-electron reduction from Fe(II)Pc to a radical Fe(II)Pc⁻ state. We identify this reduced HS Fe(II)Pc⁻ species as the critical catalytic intermediate for CO₂RR. Theoretical calculations support these findings, showing that the electroreduction of the Pc ligand shifts the d-band center of the iron atom. This electronic modification optimizes the binding energy of CO₂, thereby driving superior catalytic performance. By providing both experimental and theoretical evidence of these electronic dynamics, this work clarifies the nature of reactive sites in single-atom iron materials and offers a roadmap for the rational design of efficient CO₂ reduction catalysts.
humanized_abstract:
Single-atom catalysts (SACs) with atomically well-defined metallic sites are of great current interest because it is feasible to elucidate the sites and their relevant reaction steps. Nevertheless, the SACs research community still lacks a detailed understanding of the structures of active sites as well as the catalytic processes due to the limitation in tracing the structural and intrinsic catalytic active site dynamics under working conditions, which is mostly attributed to the lack of a highly available and site-compatible operational catalyst.

We employ advanced *operando* techniques to gain a comprehensive insight into the transient phenomena occurring during the electrochemical CO₂ reduction reaction (CO₂RR) on iron electrocatalysts. Iron centers are anchored as high-spin (HS) Fe(III)N₄ in their resting state. Using *operando* ⁵⁷Fe Mössbauer and X-ray absorption spectroscopies, we show unequivocally that upon decreasing the applied voltage, the initial HS Fe(III)N₄ coordination geometry evolves into a HS Fe(II)N₄ center. The stability of the Fe(II) species and the composition of adsorbates depend strongly on electrolyte saturation (CO₂ or Ar).

Operando Raman spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry studies confirmed that the Pc ligand of the Fe(II)Pc complex took part in the redox reactions, indicating that the ligand was reduced by one electron to form the Fe(II)Pc− radical species, and assigned the HS Fe(II)Pc− as the active intermediate of CO2RR. Further theoretical studies confirmed that the reduction of the electroactive Pc ligand moved the d-band center of iron atom, which then enhanced the CO2 binding energy, thereby resulting in superior catalytic performance. Hence, this study has demonstrated that with the aid of experimental and theoretical methodologies to reveal the electronic dynamics within the catalysts, the reaction sites of single atom iron-based catalysts in CO2RR can be well understood, and the knowledge can be utilized to guide the design and construction of the highly efficient CO2RR catalysts.

document.output:
Single-atom catalysts (SACs) with atomically well-defined metallic sites are of great current interest because it is feasible to elucidate the sites and their relevant reaction steps. Operando Raman spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry studies confirmed that the Pc ligand of the Fe(II)Pc complex took part in the redox reactions, indicating that the ligand was reduced by one electron to form the Fe(II)Pc− radical species, and assigned the HS Fe(II)Pc− as the active intermediate of CO2RR. Single-atom catalysts featuring well-defined metal centers provide a powerful platform for interrogating active site structures and the underlying mechanisms of chemical transformations.
